section.title: Judgment on Israel and Judah
section.sectionRationale: The lawsuit of chapter 4 widens. Now priests, people, and the royal house are all summoned, and Judah is named alongside Israel. The chapter exposes a nation that will bring its flocks to seek the LORD but cannot find Him, because its heart never turned, and that runs to Assyria for the healing only God can give.
section.thesis: Because a spirit of prostitution keeps Israel and Judah from truly knowing the LORD, God withdraws His felt presence and becomes to them like a moth and then a lion, until at last, broken by affliction, they admit their guilt and seek His face.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the summons and snare that entrap the whole nation; the deeds that bar the way back to God; and the LORD who wounds like a lion yet withdraws so that they will seek Him.

units.label: The summons and the snare
units.note: priests, people, and palace all charged

verses.text: “Hear this, O priests! Take heed, O house of Israel! Give ear, O royal house! For this judgment is against you because you have been a snare at Mizpah, a net spread out on Tabor.
The rebels are deep in slaughter; but I will chastise them all.
I know all about Ephraim, and Israel is not hidden from Me. For now, O Ephraim, you have turned to {{zenunim|prostitution}}; Israel is defiled.

units.label: Deeds that bar the way back
units.note: they do not know the LORD

verses.text: Their deeds do not permit them to return to their God, for a spirit of {{zenunim|prostitution}} is within them, and they do not {{yada|know}} the LORD.
Israel’s arrogance testifies against them; Israel and Ephraim stumble in their iniquity; even Judah stumbles with them.
They go with their flocks and herds to seek the LORD, but they do not find Him; He has withdrawn Himself from them.
They have been unfaithful to the LORD; for they have borne illegitimate children. Now the New Moon will devour them along with their land.

units.label: The alarm and the moth
units.note: war on the horizon, decay within

verses.text: Blow the ram’s horn in Gibeah, the trumpet in Ramah; raise the battle cry in Beth-aven: Lead on, O Benjamin!
Ephraim will be laid waste on the day of rebuke. Among the tribes of Israel I proclaim what is certain.
The princes of Judah are like those who move boundary stones; I will pour out My fury upon them like water.
Ephraim is oppressed, crushed in judgment, for he is determined to follow worthless idols.
So I am like a moth to Ephraim, and like decay to the house of Judah.

units.label: The lion who withdraws until they seek His face

verses.text: When Ephraim saw his sickness and Judah his wound, then Ephraim turned to Assyria and sent to the great king. But he cannot cure you or heal your wound.
For I am like a lion to Ephraim and like a young lion to the house of Judah. I, even I, will tear them to pieces and then go away. I will carry them off where no one can rescue them.
Then I will return to My place until they {{asham|admit their guilt}} and seek My face; in their affliction they will earnestly seek Me.”

zenunim.gloss: Prostitution, promiscuity, harlotry. In Hosea it is the standing image for Israel's covenant unfaithfulness, chasing other gods and other nations as a wife chases lovers. Here it names a settled disposition, a spirit of prostitution that lives within the people and steers them away from the LORD.

yada.gloss: To know, in the deep, relational sense of intimate acquaintance, not mere information. In Hosea to know the LORD is to live in loyal covenant fellowship with Him. Israel's failure to know Him, the same lack named in chapter 4, is the root of the ruin here.

asham.gloss: To be guilty, to bear guilt, to acknowledge one's guilt. The word can carry the whole movement from being guilty to owning it. In verse 15 God waits until Israel stops excusing itself and confesses its guilt, the turning point that opens the way back to Him.

basic.context: The court case widens to include the priests, the ordinary people, and the king's household, and even Judah is drawn in. Israel is so entangled in unfaithfulness that its very deeds keep it from returning to God. The people still bring animals to sacrifice, but they cannot find the LORD, because their hearts never turned. When they feel the wound of coming judgment, they run to Assyria for help instead of to God. So the LORD becomes like a moth quietly eating them away, then like a lion, and finally He withdraws, waiting for the day affliction drives them to seek His face.
basic.hermeneutics: This is still covenant lawsuit, moving from summons to sentence. The heaping up of place names, Mizpah, Tabor, Gibeah, Ramah, Beth-aven, roots the charges in real Israelite worship centers and battle lines. God's withdrawal is not caprice; it is measured discipline aimed at repentance, as the last verse makes plain.
basic.originalLanguages: A spirit of zenunim, prostitution, lives within the people and blocks their return. The fatal lack is again that they do not yada, know, the LORD, an intimate covenant knowing. The hope hangs on one word in the final verse, asham, to admit guilt: God waits until Israel owns its guilt and seeks His face.
basic.typology: The incurable wound that Assyria's king could not heal points to the deeper sickness of sin that no earthly power can cure, answered only in Christ the Great Physician. God's withdrawal until His people seek His face foreshadows the pattern of the gospel, where affliction drives sinners to seek and find Him in Christ.
basic.christ: Israel's wound had no cure in Assyria, and no political alliance can heal the sickness of sin. Jesus is the Physician who came for the sick, who bears our wounds and by His stripes heals us. Where God withdrew until His people would seek His face, Christ is the face of God turned toward sinners; in Him the seeking heart truly finds the LORD.
basic.god: God knows His people completely, nothing is hidden from Him. He is patient but not indulgent: He will discipline like a moth and a lion rather than leave sin to fester. Yet even His withdrawal is purposeful mercy, designed to bring a proud people to the end of themselves so they will admit their guilt and seek Him.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 9:12 shows Jesus as the Physician of the sick. Deuteronomy 31:17 warns that God will hide His face when His people forsake Him. 2 Chronicles 7:14 promises that if His people humble themselves and seek His face, He will heal.
basic.oneStory: Chapter 5 shows a people who seek God with sacrifices but never with their hearts, and cannot find Him. The whole Bible answers that impasse in Christ, in whom God turns His face toward sinners and the seeking heart at last finds the God it lost.

context.literary: A judgment oracle rich in metaphor: snare and net, moth and decay, lion and prey. It escalates from a courtroom summons to the sounding of the war trumpet, then to God as a predator, and closes on a surprising note of hope, the seeking that discipline is meant to produce.
context.historical: The oracle reflects the turmoil of Israel's last decades, when kings sought security by paying tribute to Assyria, the great king. Border towns and shrine cities are named as places of guilt. Judah is warned not to move boundary stones, a picture of injustice and land theft, and not to follow Israel's ruin.
context.canonical: The theme of God hiding His face, and of a remnant that seeks Him in affliction, runs through Deuteronomy, the Psalms, and the Prophets, and is answered in the New Testament call to seek the Lord while He may be found, fulfilled in Christ who makes God findable.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To convict priests, people, and rulers together of an unfaithfulness so deep that mere ritual cannot reach it, and to reveal that God's disciplines, even His withdrawal, aim at bringing His people to confess their guilt and seek Him truly.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes the nation's entanglement and God's just discipline; it does not commend the flight to Assyria or the empty sacrifices. What is prescriptive is the final pattern: admit guilt, seek God's face, and turn to Him rather than to human powers.

debates.question: Is God's withdrawal in verses 6 and 15 abandonment or discipline?

views.label: Disciplinary withdrawal
views.case: Verse 15 states God's purpose: He withdraws until they admit their guilt and seek His face, so the hiding is corrective, aimed at repentance and restoration.

views.label: Judicial hiddenness
views.case: The people seek Him with flocks yet cannot find Him, so the withdrawal is also a real judgment on hollow worship, a closing of the door they treated so lightly.

debates.bearing: Both are true at once: the same withdrawal that judges empty religion is also the severe mercy that drives a proud people to seek God's face in earnest, so judgment and hope are held together.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on three ideas. A spirit of zenunim, prostitution, is lodged within the people, so their unfaithfulness is not occasional but settled. Their ruin is that they do not yada, know, the LORD, the same relational lack named in chapter 4. And the whole oracle bends toward one hopeful word in the final line, asham, to admit guilt: God withdraws to His place until Israel stops making excuses, owns its guilt, and seeks His face. The imagery moves from moth, quiet inward decay, to lion, sudden tearing judgment, showing that the God who seems slow is not to be presumed upon.

typology.type: The incurable wound that Assyria could not heal
typology.fulfillment: Christ the Physician who heals the sickness of sin that no earthly power can touch, Matthew 9:12 and 1 Peter 2:24.

typology.type: God who withdraws until His people seek His face
typology.fulfillment: Christ the face of God turned toward sinners, in whom those who seek the LORD truly find Him, John 14:9.

typology.type: The lion who tears and carries off
typology.fulfillment: The just judgment fully borne by Christ, so that His people are rescued from the wrath they could not escape, Romans 5:9.

study.christ: The wound of Ephraim had no cure in the king of Assyria, and every human refuge fails the same way, because the true sickness is sin and death. Jesus is the Physician who came not for the healthy but for the sick, who was torn in our place as the Lion of judgment fell on Him, and who heals by His wounds. Where the LORD withdrew to His place until His people would seek His face, Christ is God's face turned toward us; to seek Him is to find the God whom Israel sought with flocks but never with its heart. The affliction that drives sinners to seek God finds its answer in the One who is always found by those who come.
study.god: The LORD here is the God from whom nothing is hidden, who sees Ephraim and Israel completely. He is patient, working first like a moth, slow and quiet, before He acts like a lion, and He refuses to prop up empty worship or bless alliances that replace trust in Him. Yet His severity is not the last word: He withdraws with a purpose, to bring a self-satisfied people to confess their guilt and seek His face, which is mercy wearing the mask of judgment.

crossReferences.note: Jesus, the Physician of the sick, quotes Hosea's call for mercy over sacrifice, meeting the wound Assyria could not.
God warns He will hide His face when His people turn to other gods, the withdrawal Hosea describes.
If My people humble themselves and seek My face, I will hear and heal, the pattern Hosea 5:15 sets up.
Seek the LORD while He may be found; return to Him and He will have mercy, answered fully in Christ.

study.oneStory: Chapter 5 exposes a people who bring sacrifices but not their hearts and so cannot find God. The Bible answers this impasse in Christ, in whom the LORD turns His face toward sinners, heals the wound no earthly king could cure, and is truly found by all who seek Him.

quiz.q: Why can the people not find the LORD even though they bring flocks and herds to seek Him?

options.text: Their hearts never turned; a spirit of prostitution keeps them from truly knowing God, so the ritual is empty.
options.note: Verses 4 and 6 tie their failure to find God to unfaithful hearts, not insufficient offerings.

options.text: They did not bring enough animals to sacrifice.
options.note: The quantity of offerings is not the issue; the absence of a returning heart is.

options.text: They went to the wrong shrine on the wrong day.
options.note: Location and timing are not the problem; their inner unfaithfulness is.

options.text: God had simply stopped existing for them.
options.note: God has withdrawn as discipline, not ceased to be; He waits for them to seek His face.

quiz.q: What does Israel do when it feels the wound of coming judgment?

options.text: It turns to Assyria and the great king for a cure, instead of turning to the LORD.
options.note: Verse 13 shows Ephraim seeking healing from a foreign power that cannot help.

options.text: It immediately repents and admits its guilt.
options.note: True repentance is what God waits for, but here they run to Assyria first.

options.text: It rebuilds the temple in Jerusalem.
options.note: The text describes flight to Assyria, not temple rebuilding.

options.text: It calls a national day of prayer that God accepts.
options.note: Their seeking is hollow; God withdraws rather than accepting it.

quiz.q: What is the purpose of God withdrawing to His place in verse 15?

options.text: To wait until, broken by affliction, they admit their guilt and earnestly seek His face.
options.note: Verse 15 names the goal of the withdrawal: honest confession and seeking.

options.text: To abandon them forever with no hope of return.
options.note: The withdrawal is disciplinary and hopeful, not final abandonment.

options.text: To rest because He was tired of judging.
options.note: God does not withdraw from weariness; He withdraws with a redemptive aim.

options.text: To let another god take His place among them.
options.note: There is no rival to the LORD; He withdraws to draw them back to Himself.

quiz.q: How does the incurable wound point forward to Christ?

options.text: The sickness Assyria could not heal is sin itself, cured only by Christ the Physician who heals by His wounds.
options.note: Jesus comes for the sick and bears our wounds, 1 Peter 2:24.

options.text: It shows that better doctors would have solved the problem.
options.note: The wound is spiritual; no human remedy reaches it, only Christ.

options.text: It predicts the fall of the Assyrian empire.
options.note: The point is the need Christ meets, not a forecast about Assyria.

options.text: It has nothing to do with Christ.
options.note: The incurable wound is exactly the need the gospel answers in Jesus.

quiz.q: What does the movement from moth to lion teach about God's patience?

options.text: God often works slowly and quietly first, but His patience must not be presumed upon, for judgment can come suddenly.
options.note: The moth pictures slow decay; the lion pictures sudden tearing.

options.text: God never acts in judgment at all.
options.note: The lion imagery shows real judgment does come.

options.text: God's patience means sin has no consequences.
options.note: The chapter shows patience alongside certain consequences.

options.text: God is unpredictable and arbitrary in His anger.
options.note: His discipline is measured and purposeful, aimed at repentance.

questions: The people sought God with sacrifices but not with their hearts. Where might your own religious activity be masking a heart that has not truly returned?
Israel ran to Assyria for a cure only God could give. What earthly Assyria do you turn to first when you feel the wound of your sin or trouble?
God withdrew to bring His people to admit their guilt. Is there guilt you have been excusing rather than owning honestly before God?
The wound Assyria could not heal is met in Christ the Physician. What wound do you need to bring to Him rather than to a lesser remedy?
God waits to be sought. What would it look like this week to seek His face in earnest rather than out of routine?

translationNotes.note: The New Moon may also be rendered their New Moon feast or the coming month; either way a set religious time will turn into the occasion of ruin.
Beth-aven, house of wickedness, is a derogatory name for Bethel, house of God, a center of Israel's false worship, as in chapter 4.
Determined to follow worthless idols may also be rendered determined to follow human precepts, following a variant reading of the Hebrew.
The great king may be read as the proper name King Jareb; the sense is the Assyrian monarch to whom Israel appealed for help.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Hosea 5:1-15, with poetic lines joined into running verses. Em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas or clean sentence breaks per house style.
